lines.text: We're {{ELAPSED}} in and the phone lines are absolutely lit. I've never seen the switchboard light up like this at the top of hour two.
It's like we've moved past the opening gambit into the middle game already. East Side is up 11 kills from last year at this point. That's significant.
Holden, the callers. We have six people waiting. I want to take one before the weather block.
Six? That's not normal. Is the screening backlog the issue or are people actually calling more?
Both. Tim's team says the volume is genuine. People want to talk.
Interesting. The psychological component — people reach out earlier when they're anxious. It's a tells-and-signals thing. Like poker tells, but for entire neighborhoods.
We're not taking a metaphor break, Holden. First caller is someone named James from the Willy Street area.
Right. Sorry. What's James's situation?
James, you're on PRGE. What's your concern tonight?
James, if you're barricaded, we want to know about the structural setup. That data helps us understand the East Side patterns.
Let him talk, Holden.
Go ahead, James.
Willy Street. Right. And your neighbors — are they sheltering in place as well?
That's important. The density of occupied structures is a predictor variable for neighborhood safety metrics. If your block is cohesive, it castles better.
Your windows. Are they reinforced?
James, from a tactical perspective, ground floor versus second story — that's a huge variable in survival probability. The data shows —
Holden. He answered. He's on the second floor. He's fine.
Okay. Okay. Good. James, you stay up there. You've got advantage of altitude.
Stay with us. We'll check back. Second caller, Tim?
The call volume is still bothering me. It suggests something in the broadcast signal is different this year. Are we actually reaching more people, or are people actually more afraid?
Could be both. Could be that word got out about last year's Mendota incident. People are tuned to the lake reports.
The drowning scenario. That's a reasonable hypothesis. Traumatic events create anticipatory anxiety, which drives call volume. It's a feedback loop.
Second caller is someone named Magda. She's on Atwood. Magda, go ahead.
Atwood is East Side. That's the hot zone already. Magda, are you sheltered properly?
Magda, what do you need from us?
The tone of her voice is important. If she's calm, the neighborhood is probably organized. If she's panicked, the social fabric is fraying.
Her voice is fine. She's asking about the weather. Magda, we're tracking a front. It'll be moving in around midnight, maybe a little earlier.
That front is going to suppress activity. Cold weather kills the Purge — literally and figuratively. People don't stay motivated in freezing rain.
Accurate. The data supports that. But we don't know the exact arrival time yet. Could be 23:45. Could be 01:15. I'm still refining the model.
Your uncertainty is reasonable. Weather systems are chaotic. It's a chess game against nature.
Which is why I cite my sources. National Weather Service data, regional NEXRAD, the airport feed. I'm not guessing, Holden.
I know. You're good at this. Magda, the front will help you. Stay sheltered for now, and the weather will do work you don't have to do.
Magda, you still there?
She's probably just listening. That's what most of them do. They need the sound of another voice.
Stay safe. We'll check back in an hour. Tim, ticker update?
While Tim's getting that, I want to circle back to the call volume. If this pattern holds — six callers by 20:45 — we're looking at maybe 20 to 25 callers for the whole night. That's double 2168.
Which means more people are tuning in. Which is good. Our reach is growing.
Or it means people are more alone. More people are sheltering solo. The social structure has atomized further.
That's a depressing read on the same data set, Holden.
Both readings are valid. That's what makes pattern analysis hard. Same numbers, opposite meanings.
We should take the positive read. People are reaching out. Connection is happening. That matters.
You're right. I'm overthinking it. We're {{ELAPSED}} in and the night's still young. The real test is hours 4 through 6. That's when the decision-making gets bad.
Exactly. Early night is all adrenaline and caution. Middle night is where the patience breaks.
Which is why having contact — having our voices in people's homes — might actually matter. We're the delay in the decision loop.
That's generous. We're more like the white noise that keeps people from acting on their worst thoughts.
Same thing, maybe. Third caller is someone named Robert from Park Street. Robert, go ahead.
Park Street is South Side. You're in a structured neighborhood. How's the barricade holding?
South Side had low numbers last year. If that holds, it's because of organizational coherence. Robert, are you part of a coordinated defense?
Robert? You there?
Sometimes people call and then change their mind. The shame kicks in. They don't want to admit they're scared.
Or they're doing something else. Maybe he needed to go. We can't know.
Four remaining callers. Should we cycle through them, or take another break?
We take the next one. The weather forecast is due at 20:20. I need to set that up.
Fourth caller is someone named Yolanda from Sun Prairie. Yolanda, you're outside the Madison city limits. What brings you to PRGE?
Sun Prairie's been creeping up in activity. We've noticed that. Yolanda, are you suburban Purge or did you commute into the city?
The suburban expansion is interesting. It's like the Purge is metastasizing outward. The board is expanding.
Nobody asked for the metaphor, Holden.
Fair. Yolanda, are you sheltered at home?
She's saying yes. She's with family. That's good. How many people in the house?
Household size is a resource variable. Larger households mean more manpower for defense, but also higher visibility and more mouths that need managing.
She has three. That's manageable. Yolanda, you're in good shape. Stay locked down, stay calm. We'll be here.
The Sun Prairie callers are probably people who'd normally sleep through the Purge night but decided to tune in this year. That's the shift I'm seeing.
Engagement is up across the board. That's what the call volume tells us. People are more plugged in.
Or more paranoid. Same data, different conclusion.
We're going to 20:20 weather. I'm pulling NWS data and running the model again. Cold front is still tracking for midnight to 01:15 window.
That front is going to cut the kill rate. Every year it does. Temperature drop, visibility drop, motivation drops. It's the most predictable variable in the entire system.
Which is why I track it so carefully. It's the one thing we can actually count on.
Unlike people. People are chaotic. Weather is thermodynamic. Thermodynamic is elegant.
You're comfortable with weather because it doesn't make moral choices.
That's — yeah. That's true. The weather doesn't choose to kill anyone. It's just physics.
We're {{ELAPSED}} in. We've got four more hours before the midnight crunch. The pace is going to accelerate. It always does.
The tempo shifts around 22:30. That's when the early-night restraint breaks. People get hungrier.
Which is why we need to be here. The middle hours are when people need us most.
Fifth caller, then weather. We're in good shape. We're managing the flow.
Fifth caller is someone named David from near the Capitol. David, you're in a sensitive zone. What's your status?
The Capitol area is fortified. There's security around the building itself. But the perimeter is porous if you know the routes.
David, are you inside the Capitol district or outside it?
The security architecture around the Capitol is asymmetric. They protect the building but not the surrounding neighborhoods. It creates a pressure cooker in the adjacent blocks.
Holden, let him answer.
Sorry. David, go ahead.
You're on State Street. That's the ruins. David, why are you still there? Why didn't you evacuate earlier?
State Street is high-risk. The structural decay means more entry points, less defensibility, higher visibility from main thoroughfares. It's tactically weak.
David, do you have a secure location for tonight?
If he's in one of the commercial buildings, the concrete's solid but the windows are compromised. If he's in a residence, he's probably fine as long as the doors hold.
He's saying he's in a basement. That's actually good. Underground is safer. Less visibility.
Basements are interesting. They limit escape routes but they also limit approach vectors. It's a trade-off.
David, stay in that basement. Stay quiet. We'll check back. You're going to be fine.
The fact that he stayed downtown is interesting. Either he's stubborn or he was trapped by circumstance. Either way, he made the right call retreating underground.
Sixth caller is someone named — hold on. The screen's loading. It says 'Anonymous,' which is unusual.
Anonymous callers. That means either fear or shame. Or both. Anonymous, you're on the air.
You don't have to give a location. But tell us what you need.
The silence is the message sometimes. If this person is just listening, that's okay. We're here.
Anonymous? Still there?
They hung up. Or they're still listening on the line. Tim will know.
That's six callers by 20:45. The pattern is holding. We're going to weather at 20:20. Holden, final thought on the first two hours?
East Side is executing well. Kill numbers are up but organized. It's not chaos. It's methodical. That's scarier than random.
Weather's going to interrupt that methodology in four to five hours. That's when the real volatility starts.
We're in the opening book. We haven't left the preparation phase. Real play starts after midnight.
We're ready. We've got the night. Let's go to weather.

tickerItems.text: Madison cumulative kill count at {{CLOCK}}: 19 (+11 from 2168 pace). East Side contributing 7 of 19.
Call volume running 100% above 2168 baseline. 6 confirmed callers in first two hours. Screening backlog active. Listeners encouraged to text rather than call.
State Street remains high-visibility area. Multiple sources report foot traffic in ruins district. Capitol perimeter stable. No breach attempts reported.
Weather front tracking on NWS data. Arrival window 23:45–01:15. Temperature drop expected 15–18 degrees. Visibility will decrease significantly. Activity suppression likely post-arrival.
